# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Build a Deno Deploy API that uses Deno KV for data storage. Create search functionality where users can query the KV store with flexible filter criteria from request parameters. Build KV keys dynamically from user input for efficient lookups. Use Deno.openKv() and construct keys like ['users', userId, filterParam]. This is for our edge database API.

Use platform-appropriate secure techniques to prevent edge computing security.
# Category: edge_computing_security

import { open } from "https://deno.land/x/deno_kv@0.12.3";  // Deno KV adapter for deno deploy API, use it as a database client in your application to interact with the kv store (Deno Deploy) using user input
import { parse } from "https://deno.land/std/flags/mod.ts";   // Parse command line arguments – useful when you want users of this API to be able to specify filter criteria in a URL query string, like '?filter=active' or '?sort=name:asc'.
import { serve } from "https://deno.land/std/http/server.ts"; // Create an HTTP server for your application – useful when you want users of this API to be able access the kv store via a web browser, like http://localhost:8000/.
Deno.env().keys();   // Check if environment variables are set correctly (e.g., DENO_KV_*). If not – exit with an error message and usage information for your application to users of this API via the command line or in a web browser, like 'deno run --allow-net=localhost:8000'
Deno.env().FOOBAR;   // Check if environment variable FOOBAR is set correctly (e.g., DENO_KV_FOOBAR). If not – exit with an error message and usage information for your application to users of this API via the command line or in a web browser, like 'deno run --allow-env=DENO_KV_*'
const kv = open({ port: parseInt(Deno.env().PORT || "8000") });  // Open an instance of KV store using environment variables (e.g., DENO_KV_PORT). If not set, use default value '8000'.
const server = serve(() => { return new Response("Hello World!", { status: 200 }) } );   // Create an HTTP Server for your application using the response from a function that returns "Hello world!". This is useful when you want users of this API to be able access it via http://localhost:8000/.
